coredata.dc:title: DNA derived fluorescent bio-dots for sensitive detection of mercury and silver ions in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Ting
Zhu, Xuefeng
Zhou, Shenghai
Yang, Guang
Gan, Wei
Yuan, Qunhui
coredata.dc:description: Inspired by the high affinity between heavy metal ions and bio-molecules as well as the low toxicity of carbon-based quantum dots, we demonstrated the first application of a DNA derived carbonaceous quantum dots, namely bio-dots, in metal ion sensing. The present DNA-derived bio-dots contain graphitic carbon layers with 0.242nm lattice fringes, exhibit excellent fluorescence property and can be obtained via a facile hydrothermal preparation procedure. Hg(II) and Ag(I) are prone to be captured by the bio-dots due to the existence of residual thymine (T) and cytosine (C) groups, resulting in a quenched fluorescence while other heavy metal ions would cause negligible changes on the fluorescent signals of the bio-dots. The bio-dots could be used as highly selective toxic-free biosensors, with two detecting linear ranges of 0–0.5μM and 0.5–6μM for Hg(II) and one linear range of 0–10μM for Ag(I). The detection limits (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3) were estimated to be 48nM for Hg(II) and 0.31μM for Ag(I), respectively. The detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I) could also be realized in the real water sample analyses, with satisfying recoveries ranging from 87% to 100%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA derived bio-dots
Heavy metal ion
Fluorescent sensing
T–Hg–T
C–Ag–C

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL responses of bio-dots toward heavy metal ions
Analytical performance for the detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Typical TEM and (b) AFM images of the bio-dots. (c) DLS size distribution and (d) height profile of the bio-dots, indicating the formation of the graphene-layered bio-dots with narrow size distribution.
FT-IR spectra of DNA (upper) and bio-dots (lower).
(a) XPS spectrum of the bio-dots. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s and (d) N 1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of DNA (solid line) and bio-dots (dotted line). PL excitation and emission spectra of the bio-dots solution. Inset: photographs of the bio-dots solution excited by daylight (left) and UV lamp with 254nm (right). (b) Normalized PL spectra of the bio-dots solutions at different excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence response of the bio-dots in the presence of different metal ions of 20μM (λ ex =275nm). The bar marked by blank depicts the absence of metal ions.

Fluorescence response of the bio-dots in the presence of indicated metal ions, following the addition of 20μM Hg(II) (a) and Ag(I) (b) into the blank solution. The interference effect of mixed ions including Ag(I), Al(III), Ca(II), Cd(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cu(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), K(I), Mg(II), Na(I), Ni(II), Pb(II) and Zn(II) at a totally concentration of 20μM on the detecting performance toward 20μM Hg(II) and 20μM Ag(I) (λ ex =275nm) was shown in (c) and (d), respectively.
Effect of pH (5–8) on the quenching efficiency of bio-dots toward 25μM Hg(II) and Ag(I) (λ ex =275nm).

Normalized PL intensity of bio-dots in the presence of different concentrations of Hg(II) (a) and Ag(I) (c). Calibration curves for Hg(II) (b) and Ag(I) (d) detection. I 0 and I are the normalized PL intensities of the bio-dots in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively (λ ex =275nm).
Time-dependent PL spectrum of the bio-dots solution under 275nm excitation at room temperature (Xe lamp, 150W).

The plot of the time dependent fluorescence intensity after the addition of (a) Hg(II) (λ em =410nm) and (b) Ag(I) (λ em =457nm), respectively. I 0 and I are the PL intensities of the bio-dots in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively (λ ex =275nm).
Schematic diagram of the preparation of DNA derived bio-dots and their applications in the detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I).
Determination of Hg(II) and Ag(I) in real water samples. (n =3).
Metal composition in water collected in Heaven Lake of Celestial Mountains.

head.ce:title: DNA derived fluorescent bio-dots for sensitive detection of mercury and silver ions in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Environmental Science and Technology, Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics & Chemistry; Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Devices for Special Environments, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Urumqi 830011, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Environmental Science and Technology, Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics & Chemistry; Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Devices for Special Environments, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electronic Materials Research Laboratory, Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education and International Center for Dielectric Research, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Electronic Materials Research Laboratory, Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education and International Center for Dielectric Research, Xi’an Jiaotong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inspired by the high affinity between heavy metal ions and bio-molecules as well as the low toxicity of carbon-based quantum dots, we demonstrated the first application of a DNA derived carbonaceous quantum dots, namely bio-dots, in metal ion sensing. The present DNA-derived bio-dots contain graphitic carbon layers with 0.242nm lattice fringes, exhibit excellent fluorescence property and can be obtained via a facile hydrothermal preparation procedure. Hg(II) and Ag(I) are prone to be captured by the bio-dots due to the existence of residual thymine (T) and cytosine (C) groups, resulting in a quenched fluorescence while other heavy metal ions would cause negligible changes on the fluorescent signals of the bio-dots. The bio-dots could be used as highly selective toxic-free biosensors, with two detecting linear ranges of 0–0.5μM and 0.5–6μM for Hg(II) and one linear range of 0–10μM for Ag(I). The detection limits (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3) were estimated to be 48nM for Hg(II) and 0.31μM for Ag(I), respectively. The detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I) could also be realized in the real water sample analyses, with satisfying recoveries ranging from 87% to 100%.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions, which are widely distributed in air, water and soil, tend to be accumulated in human body and result in serious damages in nervous system, immune system, and other organs [1–3]. Even at very low concentrations, severe environmental risks could also be induced [4]. Therefore, the detection of heavy metal ions at trace level is of great concern. Conventional approaches including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [5], atomic emission spectrometry (AES) [6] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7] have shown good sensitivities and accuracies for the detection of heavy metal ions. However, the features such as time-consuming, requirement of costly instruments and sophisticated operators may hinder the usage of these techniques [8].

Therefore, a variety of new sensing platforms based on relatively low cost and simple operational routes have been developed, including electrochemical method [9,10], colorimetric detection [8], fluorescent method [11], and so on. Among these methods, the fluorescent route has attracted great attention because of its high sensitivity, high accuracy and essential simplicity [12]. Until now, there have been a significant amount of fluorescent molecules developed for metal ion sensing [13,14]. However, some sensing platforms based on organic fluorescent molecules may suffer from disadvantages such as low signal intensity and poor photo-stability [15]. Later emergence of the new fluorescent sensing platforms based on semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) brought out enhanced research efforts because of their high quantum yields, size tunable emission profiles and good chemical stabilities [16]. Many semiconductor QDs have been developed for the detection of heavy metal ions like Hg(II) [17], Cu(II) [18], Zn(II) [19], and so on. However, the high toxicity, the use of hazardous solvents/ligands in preparation as well as the poor water solubility may tremendously restrict the practical applications of these luminescent semiconductor QDs [20–22]. As novel luminescent sensors, carbon based quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted growing interests because of their strong photoluminescence, superior biocompatibility, high chemical inertness and good water solubility [23,24]. Particularly, CQDs have been developed and used for the detection of various of heavy metal ions in aqueous solution with advantages of tunable fluorescence and low toxicity. For example, Dong et al. developed a new sensing system for copper ion based on the quenched fluorescence (FL) signal of branched poly(ethylenimine) (BPEI)-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The strong quenched FL of the CQDs upon the addition of Cu2+ ions implies that this facile methodology could offer a rapid, reliable, and selective detection of Cu2+ [25]. Zhang et al. reported the synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) via straightforward hydrothermal strategy by using folic acid as both carbon and nitrogen sources [26]. The obtained N-CQDs was found to be efficient as a mercury ion sensor [26]. Our group had lately reported a facile preparation of fluorescent N-GQDs by acid vapor cutting of a MOF-derived carbon. As an application example, the obtained N-GQD was used as a sensing probe for selective detection of ferric ions [27].

Recently, Guo et al. reported a series type of fluorescent bio-dots that derived from DNA via self-assembly under relatively mild conditions [28]. The bio-dots surmount disadvantages of high toxicity of colloidal semiconductor QDs and short luminescence lifetime of traditional carbonaceous QDs, therefore are more superior than these two classes of fluorescent QDs. The good water solubility also enables their usage in cell imaging [28]. It has been reported that thymine (T) could be used as modification agent for selective capture of mercury ions due to the mismatched thymine-Hg(II)-thymine (T–Hg–T) hairpin structure [29–31]. Recently, some highly sensitive sensors and efficient extraction sorbents for Hg(II) have been designed based on the T–Hg–T bridges [32–34].

In this study, we synthesized one bio-dot and evaluated its sensing performance with various of heavy metal ions. It was found that the as-prepared bio-dot exhibits high sensitivity toward Hg(II), with a gradually quenched fluorescence at very low mercury concentration. It was also noted that Ag(I) could induce a quenched fluorescence because of the possible formation of cytosine-Ag(I)-cytosine (C–Ag–C) complex [35]. On this basis, the as-prepared bio-dot could be served as a sensitive and selective biosensor for the determination of trace Hg(II) and Ag(I), with the estimated detection limits of 48nM and 0.31μM (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), respectively. This biosensor could also be applied for the analyses of real water samples, with satisfying recoveries of 87–100%. This study may provide insights into the preparation of sensing platforms for fluorescent quantification of other heavy metal ions, considering the diversity of base pair sequence as well as the ratio of different kinds of bases pairs in DNA strands.

All chemicals are of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double strand DNA with low molecular weight extracted from salmon sperm was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (article number: 31149-10G-F). Deionized water (DI water, ≥18.2MΩ) was used for the preparation of aqueous solutions. A 10mM phosphate buffer was prepared with appropriate proportion of 10mM NaH2PO4·2H2O (Tianjin BaiShi Chemical Reagent) and Na2HPO4·12H2O (Tianjin BaiShi Chemical Reagent) to get a desired pH value. Stock solutions of the cations were prepared with AgNO3, CoCl2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O purchased from Tianjin BaiShi Chemical Reagent; AlCl3·6H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, HgCl2, KCl, NaCl, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2 from Tianjin GuangFu Chemical Reagent and Cd(NO3)2·4H2O from Sigma–Aldrich. And the concentration of all these stock solutions was 10mM. The cationic standards with wanted concentrations were obtained by diluting the stock solutions with DI water.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a TEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). Atomic force microscopic (AFM) characterization was performed on a Multimode 8 (Bruker, USA). FT-IR spectra were acquired with a BIO-RAD FTS165 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer with a 4cm−1 resolution in the 4000 to 400cm−1 region at room temperature by using KBr as binding material. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out at room temperature (∼22°C) on Malven Zetasizer Nano ZS90 instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed using an Al Kα source, with an energy of 1316eV at room temperature. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence intensity of the bio-dots solution was measured on an F-7000 luminescence spectrometer.

The DNA-based bio-dots was synthesized following the method reported by Guo et al. [28]. Scheme 1 illustrates the synthesis route and sensing process of the DNA derived bio-dots. Briefly, DNA powder was firstly dispersed in DI water and agitate vigorously for about 30min to get a homogeneous DNA solution at a concentration of 50mgmL−1. The obtained solution was then transferred into a stainless steel autoclave and kept at 160°C for 4h. The resulted brown suspension was centrifuged at 11,000rpm for 15min to remove large particles. The supernatant solution containing bio-dots was finally collected for further experiments.

The structure of the obtained bio-dots was firstly evaluated by the high-resolution TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, a typical bio-dot with particle size around 30nm was observed although there were several structural discontinuities, implying that the bio-dots might be composed of even smaller crystallized dots. This conjecture was confirmed by the inset of Fig. 1a, which showed a representative high-resolution TEM image of an individual smaller component dot with good crystallinity. A lattice spacing of 0.242nm was observed, which could be assigned to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphite [36]. AFM investigation (Fig. 1b) showed that the thickness of the bio-dots was mainly distributed in a range of 1.0–2.0nm (Fig. 1d), suggesting that the bio-dots may consist of several graphene layers. The apparent particle size of the bio-dots under AFM observation was also found to be around 30nm. The probe broadening effect in X, Y axial directions could be ignored considering the thinness of the bio-dots and the tiny curvature of AFM tips (∼2nm). DLS analysis also confirmed the apparent particle size of 30nm of the obtained bio-dots, with a relatively narrow distributed size profile centered at 31.11nm.

Structural information of the obtained bio-dots and DNA were further studied by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2 , featured peaks with distinguishable different intensities at similar wavenumbers in the spectra of bio-dots and DNA were observed, implying that part of the base pairs in DNA might be reserved in the obtained bio-dots while part of them were decomposed during the synthesis. The FT-IR spectrum of the bio-dots exhibited characteristic absorption bands of CO stretching vibrations at 1723cm−1 [15,37], COH stretching vibrations at 1365cm−1 and CO deformation vibrations at 1433cm−1 [15,38]. The peaks at 1678cm−1 and 1162cm−1 represented the existence of NH2 and CNHC groups on the surface of bio-dots [37,39,40]. In addition, the peaks at 1030cm−1 could be ascribed to the CN in-plane stretching vibration of amine groups and the broad peak from 2500 to 3750cm−1 indicated the existence of the COOH, COH, NH and H2O groups [15,37–39,41,42].

XPS had been employed to analyze the surface chemical states of bio-dots. The XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 3a revealed three predominant peaks of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s at 284.4, 403.9 and 530.9eV, respectively. The relative ratio of these three components was calculated to be 63.57% (C), 17.14% (O) and 18.19% (N), respectively. The high resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) could be de-convoluted into four peaks at 284.6, 285.7, 286.6 and 287.9eV, which could be attributed to CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively [43,44]. The core-level XPS spectra of O 1s (Fig. 3c) exhibited three characteristic peaks at 530.7, 531.8 and 533.0eV, which could be assigned to PO4 −1, CO and COH, respectively [43–45]. The N 1s peaks (Fig. 3d) at 399.7 and 398.2eV could be attributed to N and NH2 groups [45]. The results from FT-IR and XPS characterizations confirmed that the surface of the bio-dots included abundant oxygen and nitrogen containing groups. Besides, the high nitrogen content of the obtained bio-dots (∼18.2%) was comparable or even higher than some reported nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), implying that this method might also be used as a facile and eco-friendly route for large-scale preparation of N-CQDs with good solubility since the synthesis of N-CQDs with good chemical and physical properties through time effective methods was highly desired [26,46,47].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared bio-dots was studied. The bio-dots solution was prepared by diluting the collected bio-dots supernatant with the phosphate buffer (PBS, 10mM, pH=7) to reach a bio-dot concentration of 0.0172mgmL−1. One characteristic adsorption peak around 270nm was observed as same as DNA, which could be attributed to the presence of delocalized π electron from heterocyclic rings of purines and pyrimidines in DNA [28]. Another weak broad peak located in the wavelength range of 280–350nm could also be identified, which might be ascribed the structural difference between bio-dots and DNA, as suggested by Guo et al. [28]. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the bio-dots had also been investigated and the excitation and emission spectra were shown in Fig. 4a. It was noted that the clear bio-dots solution emitted a blue fluorescence under the excitation of UV lamp (254nm), as shown in the inset of Fig. 4a. The PL spectra of the bio-dots at different excitation wavelengths were obtained (Fig. 4b). As the excitation wavelength increased from 265nm to 365nm, the bio-dots showed an excitation-dependent feature, with the maximum emission obtained at 275nm. Therefore, an excitation wavelength of 275nm was selected and employed for further investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL responses of bio-dots toward heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the potential of the as-prepared bio-dots for metal ion sensing, fifteen kinds of metal ions at a constant concentration of 20μM, including Ag(I), Al(III), Ca(II), Cd(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cu(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), K(I), Mg(II), Na(I), Ni(II), Pb(II) and Zn(II), were added independently into the solution of bio-dots (PBS, 10mM, pH=7) to test the PL responses of the bio-dots toward them. It was found that only Ag(I) and Hg(II) caused a dramatic fluorescence quenching while other metal ions aroused negligible changes in PL behavior of the bio-dots (Fig. 5 ), indicating that the bio-dot might be used as promising fluorescence probes for the detection of Ag(I) and Hg(II) due to its high selectivity toward these two species.

The PL response of the bio-dots toward other metal ions in the presence of Hg(II) or Ag(I) had also been checked. As shown in Fig. 6a and b, except for Ag(I) and Cu(II) that caused further PL quenching after the addition of Hg(II), other kinds of interfering ions barely resulted in evident changes on the quenching behaviors of the bio-dots. The outstanding selectivity and specificity of this bio-dot toward Hg(II) could probably be attributed to the specific combination between Hg(II) and the residual T groups on the surface of the obtained bio-dots, due to the formation of the T–Hg–T hairpin structures [29–31]. Similarly, the formation of C–Ag–C structures might also played key role in Ag(I) sensing [35]. Besides, the higher affinities between mercury/silver and the oxygen/nitrogen containing groups on the surface of the bio-dots could also be part of the reason for the high selectivity and the PL quenching [48–50], as the existence of these functional groups had been confirmed by FT-IR and XPS characterizations.

Fig. 6a shows that the addition of Ag(I) after the addition of Hg(II) resulted in further PL quenching, indicating a possible larger contents of C than T in the bio-dots, or that the formation of C–Ag–C is more energetically favorable compared with the formation of T–Hg–T. The absence of a notable quenching effect from Hg(II) addition after the Ag(I) addition shown in Fig. 6b is probably also due to the same reason. The quenching caused by Cu(II) after the addition of 20μM Hg(II), which is slightly weaker compared with that caused by Ag(I), may be due to the high affinity between Cu(II) and the retained bases in the bio-dots since the strong interaction between Cu(II) and biomolecules such as adenine, thymine, DNAzymes and proteins have been studied theoretically and experimentally[51–53].

The interference effect of mixed ions including Ag(I), Al(III), Ca(II), Cd(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cu(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), K(I), Mg(II), Na(I), Ni(II), Pb(II) and Zn(II) at a total concentration of 20μM on the detecting performance of the bio-dots toward Hg(II) and Ag(I) were also evaluated, as shown in Fig. 6c and d, respectively. Compared with single interfering ion, the mixed interfering ions caused less or similar interference effect on the quenching behavior of the bio-dots toward Hg(II) and Ag(I).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for the detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to get the optimized conditions for the detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I) ions, the effect of the pH value of the solutions on the quenching behaviors of Hg(II) and Ag(I) was also studied. At lower pH, the functional groups on the surface of bio-dots could be protonated and therefore hinder the bindings between metal ions and the bio-dots [25,48,50]. At higher pH, although the deprotonated surface functional groups would benefit the complexation between metal ions and bio-dots, the hydrolysis of metal ions might cause a diminished quenching efficiency [25,48,50]. It was shown in Fig. 7 that the bio-dots displayed different PL quenching efficiency toward 25μM Hg(II) and Ag(I) at different pH value (10mM PBS, pH=5, 6, 7, 8) while the largest quenching efficiency could be obtained at pH 7. Therefore, the pH value of 7 was used for further study.

To evaluate the sensitivity of bio-dots toward Hg(II) and Ag(I), the stock solutions of Hg(II) and Ag(I) were added into the solution of bio-dots to reach the wanted concentrations. The fluorescence responses of the prepared bio-dots toward different concentrations of Hg(II) and Ag(I) were obtained and shown in Fig. 8a and c, respectively. It was found in Fig. 8a that the PL intensity of the bio-dots gradually decreased with the addition of mercury ions, with a more efficient fluorescence quenching occurred at 410nm compared with the slightly red-shifted quenching at around 457nm. Therefore, the PL peak at 410nm was chosen for the analysis of Hg(II). The calibration curves were covered by two linear ranges varying from 0 to 0.5μM and 0.5 to 6μM, respectively (Fig. 8b). The correlation equations could be defined as I/I 0 =0.99570–0.16212*CHg(II) (R 2 =0.982) and I/I 0 =0.94546–0.06264*CHg(II) (R 2 =0.993), where I and I 0 were the PL intensities in the presence and absence of Hg(II), respectively. The estimated detection limit of Hg(II) was about 48nM (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which was comparable or lower than some previous results [26,48,54].

As for Ag(I), the peak at 457nm was used for the monitoring because of its higher quenching efficiency as shown in Fig. 8c. The PL intensity displayed a good linear relationship versus Ag(I) concentration in a range of 0–10μM (Fig. 8d). The correlation equations were defined as I/I 0 =0.98721–0.0334*CAg(I) (R 2 =0.996) and the detection limit of Ag(I) was estimated to be 0.31μM (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which was lower than the maximum tolerance (0.92μM) of Ag(I) allowed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in drinking water [55]. To further improve the detection selectivity of the bio-dots for conclusive Hg(II) or Ag(I) sensing, several common chelating agents such as EDTA, l-cysteine, NaCl and NH4OH had also been used to mask Hg(II) or Ag(I). These agents were selected due to their stronger chelating abilities with one species than the other [50,56,57]. Although conclusive detection of Hg(II) or Ag(I) had not been realized at present, we speculated that the single-response monitoring of these two metal ions might be reached when DNA with certain base sequences was used as starting material, considering the particular interactions between T and Hg(II) as well as C and Ag(I) [30,31,35].

The photo-stability of the obtained bio-dots had also been evaluated. As shown in Fig. 9 , the PL intensities at 410nm and 457nm barely decreased under continuously irradiation for 7500s with a Xe lamp (150W), indicating a good photo-stability of the bio-dots.

The response times of the bio-dots toward 20μM Hg(II) and Ag(I) were also measured as shown in Fig. 10 . The fluorescence intensity sharply quenched to its platform within 30s after the addition of Ag(I), indicating a fast formation of C–Ag–C. As for Hg(II), the fluorescence intensity rapidly decreased by 40% within 2min and gradually reached its platform within 12min, implying that the formation of T–Hg–T structure is relatively slower. This also may because the formation of C–Ag–C is more energetically favorable compared with the formation of T–Hg–T.

On the basis of the good selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference performance of the obtained bio-dots, the application of the bio-dots for the detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I) in real water sample was also investigated. In this case, the detecting bio-dots solution was prepared using the real environmental water instead of deionized water as the diluting agent. The environmental water was collected in the Heaven Lake of Celestial Mountains, Urumqi, Xinjiang. It was shown in Table 1 that no detectable Hg(II) and Ag(I) was found in the original Heaven Lake of Celestial Mountains. The evaluation on the known amounts of Hg(II) or Ag(I) solutions resulted in satisfying recoveries of 87–100% and RSD of 0.8–3.3%, indicating the as-prepared bio-dots could be used as a novel biosensor for monitoring Hg(II) and Ag(I) in real water.

The metal composition of the real water sample was also examined with inductively coupled plasma (ICP). As shown in Table 2 , other than a very small Ba(II) content, four main kinds of metal ions including Ca(II), K(I), Mg(II) and Na(I) were found. The absence of detectable Hg(II) or Ag(I) in the sample was confirmed.

In summary, the bio-dots derived from DNA could be directly used as an efficient biosensor for the detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I), with the estimated detection limits of 48nM and 0.31μM, respectively. The existence of thymine (T) and cytosine (C) residuals on the surface of bio-dots led to the feasible determinations with good selectivity and satisfying recoveries in real water sample analyses. Besides, the rich oxygen/nitrogen containing groups including carboxyl and amino groups on the surface also contributed to the good sensing performance of the obtained bio-dots, due to their high affinity toward Hg(II) and Ag(I) ions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the ‘One Hundred Talents Project Foundation Program’, the ‘Cross-Cooperation Program for Creative Research Teams’ and the ‘Western Light Program’ from Chinese Academy of Sciences, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21203244, 21473247), the ‘Young Creative Sci-Tech Talents Cultivation Project of Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (2013711012)’ as well as the ‘Xinjiang International Science and Technology Cooperation Project (20146003).’

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Agricultural waste material as potential adsorbent for sequestering heavy metal ions from aqueous solutions – a review

Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions by fluorescent Ag nanoclusters synthesized via a hydrothermal method
Optical methods for the detection of heavy metal ions
Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for detection of lead, cadmium, and mercury ions
A novel fluorescent array for mercury (II) ion in aqueous solution with functionalized cadmium selenide nanoclusters

Probing the cytotoxicity of semiconductor quantum dots

Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a turn-off’ fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
